Three Ways to Drive Traffic

TRANSCRIPT / BONUS / Promoting Other People’s Launches
TRANSCRIPT
Okay, now I want to drill down into some of the tactics and the tactical questions on
how you promote other people's launches as a Joint Venture partner or as an affiliate
partner. There's three primary ways that you do that, that you drive traffic and sales.
I'm going to be walking through the three of those.
First of all, we have email and social. This is a favorite of mine because I've got a
big email list and I've got a big social following, and I can drive a ton of traffic, so
this is pretty much what I use. If you've got an email list, I suggest you do the same.
It works really, really well. I love selling via email. I love publishing via email. It's just
the killer application still after all these years, and there's no sign of it changing.
Social's the up-and-comer. If you've got a great social following, that's fantastic, but
still, I think in general, email is the stronger of the two. That's basically it. You've got a
following, you've got a platform, and you use that platform.
Number two is paid traffic. As I record this, and it might change in the future, the
near future or the distant future, but the winner right now is Facebook. A few
years ago it was more Google AdWords, now it's Facebook. YouTube is also a really
good one right now. The platforms change over time, but the whole idea is where you
spend money to drive traffic into someone else's launch. This can be really, really
effective. I have done this when I've promoted other people's launches, it worked
really well. I've had people promote my launches primarily using paid traffic and had
it work really well.
Obviously, you're spending money, so there is some risk here. It takes some real
knowledge to be able to do this. If you're just starting out, probably not the thing to
do. If you're sending them an unproven offer, probably not the thing to do because
the thing is you're going to be spending money, sending traffic into launch, and not
knowing if it converts until the launch closes. Be careful with this one. It can work
really well, but be careful.
The third one, this is a great one if you're just starting out, and I've seen it used
very effectively, is just direct referrals, or we could say personal referrals. This is
where you just ... you're telling your associates, you're telling people you know, it
could even be friends, it could be family members if it's a great fit. It's more of a one-
on-one experience where you're going to them, you're telling about it and saying,
"This things awesome, and by the way, if you use this link, I'll get credit for it." Maybe
you can give them some additional coaching or whatever, or just say, "Hey, I really
think you should get this thing, it's amazing. Here, use this link."
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

TRANSCRIPT / BONUS / Promoting Other People’s Launches
The thing about direct referrals, I seen it work really, really well for people
promoting Product Launch Formula®. I look, and they might have sent 16 or 18
visitors, but they drove 8 sales and they made thousands of dollars in commissions.
This is one that no matter where you're at when you're first starting out, you can
probably do this. If you have some acquaintances, some associates, some people
that you work with where it's more almost a one-on-one sale. If you look at ...
I mean, at Product Launch Formula we paid some major league commissions. If the
product costs $2000, we're very generous with our commissions. If you just have
three or four friends that you tell about the product, you could end up doing a lot of
money in commissions. Of course that's Product Launch Formula it worked. Whatever
product, whatever market you’re promoting, direct referrals can be really
powerful. I've seen people hit our top 20 in my leader board, and they do it
basically just through direct referrals. This could be a great place to start out, great
place to start. Especially, if it's a good prelaunch and you're driving people into that
prelaunch to get good content.
That's the overall, the three big ones. Now, let's step back and go through them now.
If you're going to promote via email and social, you're basically going to want to drive
people into the prelaunch. I'll do a little bit of a deep dive into exactly how to do this
in one of the later videos. It's basically, you're creating copy that drives people into
the prelaunch, and then usually you want to mail each piece of the prelaunch and
then mail at Open Cart and mail at the Close Cart, and that's the 10,000-foot long
view. Like I said, I'll drill into this a little bit more. How do you drive them in there? This
becomes a copy question.
Generally, when you're promoting something, if you can talk about your personal
experience with either the product or the company or the person who created
the product, that could be really great. If you can tell a personal story that leads into
the prelaunch, like I mentioned in the last video, I recently promoted one of Eben's
products, and it's a product I went through several years ago, and it's made a very
large impact on my life. That was a personal experience. That became the overall
theme of my entire, my support. It's like a had a prelaunch for the prelaunch. It was
like during the prelaunch I was mailing the different videos, and I was talking about
my personal experience and what I took away from it and why it was so important.
Then I would tie that into something that was going to be shared in that piece of
Prelaunch Content, that video. I would make my story relevant to the piece of
Prelaunch Content to get people to go opt in.
I knew that Eben would do a great follow-up, and he would follow-up with emails
once people got on the Launch List, and would follow-up all the way through. It's my
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 6 --

TRANSCRIPT / BONUS / Promoting Other People’s Launches
job to get people onto his list, and so I told a story that tied into each video. It was like
the story of my morning ritual. It's really, really powerful if you can build in your
experience or your story. It doesn't mean that you had to go through the product and
have that direct experience like I did. It could be an experience around the topic area.
It could be an experience of the company. It could be an experience of the product
creator. You basically want to send people into each piece of Prelaunch Content, then
send people to the Open Cart, then send them to some additional … if you can send
them some additional content during the cart week, and then start messaging scarcity
right before the close, and then really focus on that close.
Generally the way one of these ... when you're supporting one of these, just like when
you have a launch, most of the sales come right at the end. You'll get people buying
at Open Cart, and then some people trickling in during the week, but then right at the
end, the scarcity, right before the close, that really drives your sales. I will generally, if
the launch is going well, I'll mail two times the day of the Cart Close, sometimes even
three times that day of the Cart Close. Usually I'll see the number of sales I make.
Whatever the sales are the day before the close, I'll double that or triple that on the
day of the close. Now, the other thing you want to do is offer a bonus. Offering a
bonus, you don't have to do this every time, but offering a bonus, it sets you
apart, and it just makes the offer more compelling, more personal. It gives you a
reason to mail.
Paid traffic, it's hard to share that experience. It's hard to share that story because
what you're doing is you're running a small ad that's driving people into the
prelaunch. If you're just, like, 10 or 15 words on Facebook to drive them into the
Prelaunch Content, you really don't have that capability to tell your own story here. At
that point, you’re limited to using your skill in buying adds and driving traffic right into
a squeeze page, an opt-in page that then puts people on the Launch List, and then
you're reliant on that launch process to make the sale. They're a little bit less
experienced there.
Going back to direct referrals, it's very much like email and social in that you can tell
the story, but here you're telling that story one-on-one to your associates, to your
contacts. Again, this is generally about small numbers and high conversions. Basically
number one and number three here, you're telling your story, you're telling your
experience, you're basically giving them a reason why. Number two here is more
you're just delivering the hook in a very, very small ad. That hook is to try to get them
to click to go through to the opt-in page and then opt in.
One of the things when you're looking at what are your tactics going to be in a
launch, and this really applies very much so especially to bonuses, and I'll talk about

TRANSCRIPT / BONUS / Promoting Other People’s Launches
that in the next video. One of the absolutely most important things is look at your
competition. In other words, if I'm in a launch and I'm supporting something, let's go
back to Eben, I've been talking about Eben. I'm promoting a launch by Eben Pagan, a
good friend of mine, puts out great products, get great support in the market. Now,
when I'm mailing for Eben, I know that many other, the people that are on my list,
remember they're on other people's list as well. The other people, the other lists their
on are my peers in this market, and my peers are probably going to be promoting
Eben as well.
If you think about it, let's just do it, here's Eben right here, okay. Here's me, I'm right
here. I'm going to be sending my traffic to Eben. Now, what I have to think about is
the people on my list, are they on these other lists? Whoever they are, all these
different people, my people are on all these folks lists, and I know if they're going to
end promoting for Eben, then I go into competitive mode. I know that I am
competing against these guys for that sale. It basically will often come down to
bonuses and whether I can put together a better bonus package, or I can just put
together a better story around this promotion. In that case, I really have to think
hard about what are these folks doing. I get on their list, I look at what kind of
bonuses they're doing, and I'm competing against their bonuses. That is very, very
much on my mind.
Let's just say this isn't Eben. Let's just say this is someone that is not on any of their
radar. Let's just say a friend of mine, Michael Hyatt, is doing a launch, and I just know
that these guys, they're just not going to mail for him for whatever reason because
he's a little bit outside of the market, outside of what they consider their market. Now,
I look at my list, and I know a lot of people are mailing for Michael, but the folks that
are on my list are not on those other person's lists. Let's just say here's affiliate X, and
here's affiliate Y, and here's affiliate Z. I know they're going to be mailing for Michael
as well, but in general the overlap between my list and their list is not very great. Now
I'm sort of in a vacuum, I'm promoting in a vacuum. That means I don't have to give
quite as amazing bonus. You see where I'm going here? It really will affect my strategy
of what I mail and how I mail and what I mail and what I give as a bonus.
Maybe these people, they're not going to mail at the end of the launch. I know I can
look on the leaderboard if I want to compete and hit the top of the leaderboard, I can
look and say, "Well, I've got a feeling this person, they haven't done as many
launches, they don't know how to promote these. They're probably not going to mail
a couple times the last day. I know I'm going to mail a couple times the last day, so I
can catch them on that leaderboard." There are a lot of subtleties here, and really, I'm
probably going a little too deep on this for many people, especially if you're just
going to do some direct referrals.

TRANSCRIPT / BONUS / Promoting Other People’s Launches
That is something to think about. I'm a big fan of cooperation and really working
with my partners. I don't think in terms of competitors, I think of partners and
future partners. This is one situation where there is a little bit of competition for that
leaderboard, and I want to do as well as I can on that leaderboard just to build my
position in that marketplace and to show my value to the people I'm supporting, and
so I think about these types of things. It's like, "Am I more operating in a vacuum, or
are the people on my list also on the list of other people that are promoting the same
launch?" That will very much inform the strategy that you end up using.
That's some of the tactics. Like I said, I'm going to do deep dive and show you exactly
how to do this in another video. In the next video I'm going to talk about bonus
strategy.
